text: Hello and welcome to This Is My Architecture.
I'm here with Shrapria from Cloud Health by VMware.
Hi, Shrapria.
Hi, thanks for having me here.
Yeah, we're happy to have you.
Can you tell me a little bit about Cloud Health by VMware?
Sure.
So Cloud Health Secure State is a cloud security
and monitoring platform.
We help our customers mitigate security and compliance risks
through our intelligent real-time insights.
That's great.
I'm really looking forward to diving in
and learning a little bit more.
So can you start where we would do the collection piece?
Sure.
So we have primarily two modes of collection.
The main aspect, like I mentioned,
is the real-time detection of security violations.
So for that, we leverage the cloud providers' native security
event streams, which is like CloudWatch or activity log
in case of Azure.
And once the events are coming into our system,
we have a collection framework which evaluates those events
and determines the current state of the resource
and publishes those resources into a Kinesis data stream.
Awesome.
So as an architect, what challenges
would you face in this architecture?
Right.
So one of the main challenges, as with any data pipeline,
is the scale that we have to handle, right?
So we are supporting three major cloud providers today.
And each of these cloud providers have a lot of services
that we also support.
So given that, we are talking about billions of events
per day.
And that scale is pretty huge to handle.
And with that, topped by the number of resources
we collect and monitor.
And to provide real-time insights,
we have to exercise a graph-based exploration
on those resources.
So those are the primary challenges we have in our system.
Yeah.
No, that's great.
So you mentioned the graph-based piece.
I'm curious to hear how Neptune plays in.
Sure.
So once the cloud resources are ingested into our data stream,
we have the consumer, which publishes those resources
into Amazon Neptune, right?
So the primary usage of Amazon Neptune
is a graph database, like we all know.
And the cloud uses data is a highly interconnected data set.
And we wanted to provide our customers graph-based exploration
capabilities.
Neptune allows us to do that efficiently.
And on top of that, it also provides a read scale-out that
we require for executing a lot of rules on them,
on the cloud resources.
And we also chose Neptune because it's schema-less.
So one of the key considerations in our system
is as we onboard more cloud providers,
support more resource type, each of them
have varied configurations.
And we want our data layer to be agnostic of those
representations.
And that is another reason that we had to choose Neptune.
Yeah.
No, that makes sense.
How many assets are we talking about here?
Yeah.
I think in terms of, like I said,
we are handling billions of events, which
amounts to about 50 million-plus resources
that we collect and monitor in our pipeline.
And this number is an ever-growing number.
Yeah.
Wow.
That's a lot.
That's a lot of assets to process.
So after the data goes into Neptune, what happens next?
Right.
So I think we also wanted to provide our customers
capability to slice and dice this cloud resources data
in various forms and shape.
We want them to have reporting analytical capabilities,
search aggregations.
And also we want them to visualize how the resource configurations
have changed over time.
So one thing we found out is there is no one database that
can perform all these more efficiently.
So apart from Neptune, which is our primary persistence,
we do have Elasticsearch and DynamoDB as our backing
stores to power some of the other use cases.
Yeah.
No, that makes sense.
Those are great to use to process that.
But I am very curious to hear more about this area over here.
Why are we using sharding?
Sure.
So I think Neptune provides the read scale out
that we require to exercise and execute the rules.
We also wanted a strategy to scale our writes out.
And the way we do that is with our sharder service.
So sharder service basically provides us a logical grouping
of data that we want to store in our provision Neptune
clusters.
And the sharder service is intelligent in itself
that it uses this CloudWatch metrics from Neptune
and Elasticsearch to kind of determine the shard capacity
of these provision Neptune clusters.
So it uses CPU memory and few other parameters to determine,
OK, given Neptune is full or not.
And that allows us to efficiently load balance our customers' data
in the provision Neptune clusters.
So that information kind of flows back, which
allows us to make decisions at this front.
Yeah.
No, that's really smart use.
So once that data is sharded and put back into Neptune,
where does it go?
Sure.
So once the data is all persisted, the next thing in our pipeline
is the rule executions, right?
So the incoming event along with the resource changes
are then propagated to our rule execution pipeline.
The rule engine that we have is smart enough
to determine what rules needs to be executed for a given event
and the resource changes.
And this could be like thousands of rules, right,
that gets executed.
So we fronted with the Kinesis data stream.
And we exercise this over the Neptune graph database
to perform those security checks.
And if it detects any security violations,
those are again persisted in our DynamoDB.
And also we provide real-time notifications to our customers
through various channels.
We have support for Slack, email, Splunk, and the like.
That's awesome.
That's really great for your customers.
Shafriya, thank you so much for joining us today.
I've learned a lot about CloudHealth SecureState.
Thank you.
And thank you for watching This Is My Architecture.
